title: Read the Community Site State
summary: Look at an initialized community-site repository that still has five untracked project files. Learn to read Git status and short-status, stage the five exact project paths, and inspect the staged snapshot (what Git would record) before committing.

sketchBlocks.title: Read untracked files with git status and the short view
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example:

You have a repository folder with several files that Git has never seen. To learn what Git would record, start by asking Git for the status. Example commands (illustrative only):

```
# show the normal, human-readable status
git status

# show the compact two-column short status
git status --short
```

Imagine git status prints a list of untracked files and git status --short shows lines that begin with `??` for each one. The short output is useful because each two-character prefix tells you both the staged and working-tree state for a file: the first column shows the staged state (A, M, D, or a space) and the second column shows the working-tree changes. When you see `?? file.txt`, that means Git considers file.txt untracked — it is present in the working tree but not staged and not in any commit yet.

Interpretation: an untracked file will not be recorded by a commit until you stage it. Short status makes it easy to scan many files quickly and confirm whether the five project paths you care about are still untracked or already staged.

sketchBlocks.title: Move the same five files from "untracked" into the staging area
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Staging copies the current working-tree contents into the index (staging area) so that those exact versions will be recorded by the next commit. Use git add with the file paths you want to include in a proposal.

A compact workflow:

- Run git status --short to see which files are untracked (look for `??`).
- Use git add <path> (or list of paths) to stage them.
- Re-run git status --short: staged additions show up with `A ` in the first column.

Worked small example (explain): if `git status --short` changes from `?? README.md` to `A  README.md` after git add README.md, that means README.md is now staged and will be part of the next commit. The first-column `A` is the important signal: the index contains a new file. This lets you stage exactly the five project paths you intend to commit next, preventing accidental inclusions.

sketchBlocks.title: View exactly what the staged snapshot would record
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
After staging files, the staging area contains the exact contents that a commit will record. To inspect that snapshot without creating a commit, use git diff --staged (also known as --cached) and tools like git diff --staged --name-only.

What to look for:

- git status --short shows which paths are staged (first-column markers). For staged additions you typically see `A  path`.
- git diff --staged shows the differences between HEAD (what's recorded) and the staging area. For a brand-new repository with no commit yet, the staged files appear as new file additions in the diff output.

Interpretation: git diff --staged lets you double-check file contents and prevents committing the wrong content. Use it if you want to confirm that the snapshot contains the exact lines you expect before running git commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Translate short-status and staged diff into the commit you will make
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Before you commit, combine the short status and staged diff to predict the commit record.

- Short status (`git status --short`) tells you which files are staged and which remain untracked. Look at the first column for staged markers.
- The staged snapshot (`git diff --staged`) shows the exact content differences that the forthcoming commit will capture.

Common pitfall: forgetting to stage a file (it remains `??`) or staging a file you didn't mean to include. The inspect-change-verify habit (status → add → status → diff --staged) prevents both mistakes and gives you confidence about what your first commit will contain.

quizDraft.title: Inspect and stage five community-site files (set A)
quizDraft.prompt:
The workspace contains an initialized community-site repository with five untracked project files in community-site/: homepage.html, team.md, events.md, style.css, and README.md. Using the terminal, stage exactly those five files so they appear in the staging area. Then run git status --short and git diff --staged --name-only to confirm the staged snapshot lists those five paths. Type each command and press Enter.

Expected verification: git status --short shows each path with `A ` in the first column, and git diff --staged --name-only prints the five file paths (one per line).
quizDraft.starterCode:
# The repository and files are ready in the workspace.
# Use terminal commands to inspect and stage the five files.
# Example checks you might run (do not copy as the final check):
# pwd
# ls community-site
# git status --short

# Type the git commands and press Enter in the terminal to perform the task.

starterFiles.content:
# Community Site
Short description of the project.

solutionFiles.content:
# Community Site
Short description of the project.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git status --short
# stage the five specific project files (type the command and press Enter)
git add community-site/homepage.html community-site/team.md community-site/events.md community-site/style.css community-site/README.md
# verify the index and staged snapshot
git status --short
git diff --staged --name-only
quizDraft.hint: Start by listing the workspace, then stage the five files with git add. Re-check with git status --short and git diff --staged --name-only.

help.concept: Staging copies the working-tree file contents into the index so the next commit will record those exact files. git status --short shows staged files with an `A` in the first column; git diff --staged shows what will be recorded.
help.hint_1: Run git status --short to see `??` markers for untracked files, then use git add with the five community-site paths.
help.hint_2: After staging, git status --short should show lines starting with `A ` for each of the five paths and git diff --staged --name-only should list exactly those paths.

quizDraft.title: Stage five community-site files (set B) and read short status
quizDraft.prompt: This exercise uses a different set of five project files in community-site/: index.html, about.md, contact.md, main.css, and CONTRIBUTING.md. Stage exactly those five files so the staging area contains them. Then show git status --short and git diff --staged --name-only to confirm your staged snapshot. Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# The repository is initialized and the files exist in community-site/.
# Use the terminal: inspect with ls and git status --short, then stage the exact five files.
# Type commands and press Enter to run them.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
git status --short
# stage the required five files (type the command and press Enter)
git add community-site/index.html community-site/about.md community-site/contact.md community-site/main.css community-site/CONTRIBUTING.md
# confirm the staging area
git status --short
git diff --staged --name-only
quizDraft.hint: List the folder, then add the five exact paths. Re-run git status --short to see `A ` for each staged file.

help.concept: git add places working-tree contents into the index. Short status shows staged files with an `A` in the first column; git diff --staged --name-only lists the files staged for the next commit.
help.hint_1: Use git add with the five community-site paths (index.html, about.md, contact.md, main.css, CONTRIBUTING.md).
help.hint_2: After staging, git status --short should show lines beginning with `A ` for those five files; git diff --staged --name-only should print the same paths.

quizDraft.title: Stage five community-site files (set C) and inspect staged diff
quizDraft.prompt:
Stage these five community-site files: welcome.html, mission.md, volunteers.md, site.css, and README.md. After staging them, run git status --short and git diff --staged to inspect what content will be recorded. Type each command and press Enter.

Expected verification: git status --short shows `A ` for each of the five files; git diff --staged prints the additions (new-file hunks) for those files.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Files exist under community-site/. Use the terminal to stage and inspect the snapshot.
# Helpful checks: git status --short and git diff --staged
# Type each command and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# Community Site
Short description of the project.

solutionFiles.content:
# Community Site
Short description of the project.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git status --short
# stage the required five files (type the command and press Enter)
git add community-site/welcome.html community-site/mission.md community-site/volunteers.md community-site/site.css community-site/README.md
# confirm
git status --short
git diff --staged
quizDraft.hint: Use git add with the listed paths, then check with git status --short and git diff --staged.

help.concept: git diff --staged displays the differences between the index and HEAD — exactly the changes that will be recorded by the next commit. For new files it shows the new-file additions.
help.hint_1: Stage the files with git add community-site/welcome.html community-site/mission.md community-site/volunteers.md community-site/site.css community-site/README.md.
help.hint_2: After staging, git status --short should show `A ` for each path and git diff --staged should include the new-file contents for those files.

quizDraft.title: Stage five community-site files (set D) including a nested path
quizDraft.prompt:
Stage these five community-site paths: home.html, team.txt, contact_us.md, styles/main.css, and README.md (note one file is in a styles/ subfolder). After staging, confirm with git status --short and git diff --staged --name-only. Type each command and press Enter.

Expected verification: git status --short shows `A ` for each path (including the nested styles/main.css), and git diff --staged --name-only lists the five paths.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Files are present under community-site/, including a styles/ subfolder.
# Use the terminal to add the exact paths and then confirm the staged snapshot.
# Type commands and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# Community Site
Short description of the project.

solutionFiles.content:
# Community Site
Short description of the project.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git status --short
# stage the five exact paths (type the command and press Enter)
git add community-site/home.html community-site/team.txt community-site/contact_us.md community-site/styles/main.css community-site/README.md
# confirm staging
git status --short
git diff --staged --name-only
quizDraft.hint: Remember to include the subfolder path exactly: community-site/styles/main.css. After git add, check git status --short and git diff --staged --name-only.

help.concept: Git stages files by path. Paths inside subfolders must be given exactly to git add. The short status and staged diff both reflect those exact paths.
help.hint_1: Run git add community-site/home.html community-site/team.txt community-site/contact_us.md community-site/styles/main.css community-site/README.md.
help.hint_2: Verify with git status --short (first column `A `) and git diff --staged --name-only to list the staged paths.

quizDraft.title: Why local version control matters
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following best explains why a local Git repository is preferable to keeping duplicate final folders (like copy_v1, copy_v2)?
options: Git keeps a compact, readable history of changes so you can see and restore exact changes without many full duplicate folders.
Git makes a separate full copy of your project for each save so you have multiple folders to compare manually.
Using duplicate folders is faster and safer than Git because it avoids learning commands.
Git automatically uploads every folder copy to a remote server so you never need local backups.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what Git tracks and how history is reconstructed.

help.concept: Git records changes as a chain of snapshots and metadata rather than storing multiple full copies of a project. This makes history smaller, easier to read, and safer to manage than many duplicate final folders.
help.hint_1: Git stores precise snapshots and change metadata so you can see what changed and why without keeping multiple full copies.
help.hint_2: Look for the option that mentions clear history, smaller storage, and safer tracking of changes rather than manual folder copying.

quizDraft.title: Meaning of the short-status `??` marker
quizDraft.prompt: In the output of git status --short, what does a line beginning with `??` for a path mean?
options: The path is untracked — Git has not staged it and it is not in any commit.
The path is staged for deletion and will be removed by the next commit.
The path is modified in the working tree but the index still has the previous version.
The path is staged and ready to commit.
quizDraft.hint: Which column describes staged vs. working-tree state in the short status?

help.concept: In git status --short the two-character prefix encodes staged state (first column) and working-tree changes (second column). `??` indicates the file is untracked (not staged and not in any commit).
help.hint_1: The `??` marker appears when Git has never tracked the file and it is not in the index.
help.hint_2: It means the file is present in the working tree but will not be included in a commit unless you git add it.

quizDraft.title: Which outputs indicate a file has been staged?
quizDraft.prompt: Select all outputs or signs that indicate a file is staged (present in the index) and will be included in the next commit.
options: git diff --staged shows a diff hunk for README.md
git diff (unstaged) shows a change for README.md
quizDraft.hint: Look for both short-status markers and staged-diff behavior.

help.concept: Staged files are shown by an `A` (added) or `M` (modified) in the first column of git status --short and they appear in git diff --staged output because the index differs from HEAD.
help.hint_1: Check the first column of git status --short (it shows the index/staged state) and whether git diff --staged prints the file's changes.
help.hint_2: Pick the options that reference the first-column markers and the staged diff listing, not only working-tree modifications.

quizDraft.title: Correct order to inspect and stage before committing
quizDraft.prompt: Put these actions in the order you would normally perform them to inspect and stage a set of files before making a commit.
tokens: Run git status (or git status --short) to list untracked files
Use git add with the exact file paths you want to include
Run git status --short again to see staged markers (first column)
Run git diff --staged to inspect the snapshot that will be recorded
correctOrder: Run git status (or git status --short) to list untracked files
Use git add with the exact file paths you want to include
Run git status --short again to see staged markers (first column)
Run git diff --staged to inspect the snapshot that will be recorded
quizDraft.hint: Start by seeing what Git currently shows, then add files, then confirm the staged snapshot.

help.concept: A safe inspect-and-stage habit is: check status, add files, re-check status, then inspect the staged snapshot (diff --staged) before committing.
help.hint_1: The first step is to look at git status to find untracked files.
help.hint_2: After staging, inspect the index with git status --short and git diff --staged to confirm what will be committed.

quizDraft.title: Fill the short-status blank: untracked marker
quizDraft.prompt: Fill the blank: The short-status marker for an untracked file is [blank1]
quizDraft.template: The short-status marker for an untracked file is [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Short-status uses two characters; untracked files have a distinctive two-character marker.

help.concept: git status --short uses `??` to mark untracked files explicitly. This is different from staged markers like `A` or modified markers like `M`.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that is a two-character question-mark pair.
help.hint_2: Remember: `??` means Git has never tracked that path.

quizDraft.title: Fill the blank: capture staged changes into history
quizDraft.prompt: Fill the blank: To capture staged changes into history, run git [blank1]
quizDraft.template: To capture staged changes into history, run git [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Which command creates a new the missing term from the staged contents?

help.concept: git the missing term creates a new the missing term object from the current index (staging area) and records it in the repository history (HEAD).
help.hint_1: Staging is done with git add; the next command to record it is git the missing term.
help.hint_2: Choose the command that creates a new the missing term object from what's in the index.
